reflections.title: The Soldier Who Wept for His Enemy
reflections.oneLiner: At the peak of Roman power, a general watches Carthage burn and sees Rome's own future.

reflections.title: The Armor Karna Gave Away
reflections.oneLiner: Born with divine armor fused to his skin, Karna gave it away knowing it would cost him his life.

reflections.title: The Vulture King Who Fell for Dharma
reflections.oneLiner: An ancient vulture king fought Ravana alone — not because he could win, but because looking away wasn't an option.

reflections.title: What Marcus Aurelius Wrote in the Dark
reflections.oneLiner: The most powerful man in the world spent his nights writing private notes to himself — reminders not to waste the morning, not to be angry, not to fear death.

reflections.title: The God Who Forgot What He Was
reflections.oneLiner: The most powerful person in the room didn't know he was the most powerful person in the room — until someone simply reminded him.

reflections.title: The King Who Chose Exile Over a Crown
reflections.oneLiner: A warrior prince at the peak of his power chose dharma over desire — and walked into fourteen years of exile without once calling it sacrifice.

reflections.title: The Night Rage Burned Everything
reflections.oneLiner: After the war was already over, one man's grief-fueled rage destroyed more than the entire eighteen-day battle — and earned him the only curse in the Mahabharata with no expiration date.

reflections.title: The Man Who Read Plato Before He Died
reflections.oneLiner: Rather than accept Caesar's mercy and live as a symbol of conquered ideals, Cato read Plato twice, then chose his death.

reflections.title: The Vulture Who Sat Wingless for Decades
reflections.oneLiner: Sampati burned his wings saving his younger brother Jatayu, sat forgotten on a cliff for decades, and then gave the monkey army the one piece of information that let the Ramayana continue.

reflections.title: The Brother Who Didn't Sleep for Fourteen Years
reflections.oneLiner: Indrajit had a boon making him unkillable by anyone who slept or ate like a normal man — the one warrior shaped exactly like that loophole wasn't even trying to be.
